info.description: This set of questions contain a constructed patient's history, which serves to give an example of how a disease may be manifested. The questions should be done in order (that is no skipping), in order to maximize learning value.
info.persistentInformation:
A 28-year-old man presents to the clinic with a painless lesion on his penis that he noticed 10 days ago. He reports recent unprotected sexual contact with a new partner. He denies dysuria, urethral discharge, or systemic symptoms. 
	On examination, there is a single, well-demarcated ulcer with a clean base and indurated borders located on the shaft of the penis. The lesion is non-tender. Bilateral, non-tender inguinal lymphadenopathy is present.
	Dark-field microscopy of fluid obtained from the lesion demonstrates motile spirochetes.

q1.prompt: What is most likely the diagnosis?
correctOptionIds: Acute rheumatic fever following untreated streptococcal pharyngitis

q2.prompt: What is the most likely causative organism, and what is its morphology on Gram stain?

q3.prompt: What is the hemolytic pattern of this organism? Which test differentiates it from other organisms with similar morphology and hemolysis, and what is the expected result?
correctOptionIds: β-hemolytic; bacitracin sensitivity test; organism is bacitracin-sensitive

q4.prompt: This organism can cause a toxic shock-like syndrome. Which toxin is responsible for this?

q5.prompt: What are the two major nonsuppurative sequelae of infection with this organism, and which type of infection is each associated with?
correctOptionIds: Acute rheumatic fever, follows pharyngitis only; Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis, follows pharyngitis or skin infection (impetigo)

q6.prompt: What is the treatment of choice for pharyngitis caused by this organism?

q7.prompt: What virulence factors of this organism promote immune evasion?
correctOptionIds: Hyaluronic acid capsule: interferes with phagocytosis. M protein: blocks the binding of complement compoent C3b -> impair phagocytosis.

q8.prompt: Explain the pathophysiology of this patient's condition and identify the type of hypersensitivity reaction involved.
correctOptionIds: Molecular mimicry: antibodies against M protein cross-react with host tissues (heart, joints, CNS), leading to inflammation; this is a type II hypersensitivity reaction

q9.prompt: [Extra] Why are antistreptolysin O (ASO) titers used instead of antibodies to streptolysin S? Name one additional laboratory test that can detect prior infection.
correctOptionIds: Streptolysin O is antigenic, therefore can cause the body to produce antibodies against this toxin. On the other hand, streptolysin S is not antigenic, so there is no production of antibodies against this toxin, so it cannot be used as a diagnostic test for past *Streptococcus pyogenes* infections. Some other diagnostic tests are anti-DNase B and anti-hyaluronidase for example.
